title: What is the difference between var, let, and const?
answer: var is function-scoped and can be redeclared, often leading to bugs. let is block-scoped and can be reassigned but not redeclared in the same scope. const is block-scoped and cannot be reassigned after initialization (though objects/arrays it holds can still be mutated).

title: What is the difference between == and === in JavaScript?
answer: == compares values after type coercion (converting operands to a common type before comparing), while === compares both value and type without coercion, making it the safer, more predictable choice in almost all cases.

title: What are the primitive data types in JavaScript?
answer: JavaScript has seven primitive types: string, number, boolean, null, undefined, symbol, and bigint. Everything else — objects, arrays, functions — is a reference type.

title: What is hoisting in JavaScript?
answer: Hoisting is JavaScript's behavior of moving variable and function declarations to the top of their scope during compilation. var declarations are hoisted and initialized as undefined; let and const are hoisted but remain in a 'temporal dead zone' until their actual declaration line runs.
example:
console.log(x); // undefined, not an error
var x = 5;

title: What is the difference between null and undefined?
answer: undefined means a variable has been declared but not yet assigned a value. null is an intentional assignment representing 'no value' — a developer explicitly sets it to indicate emptiness.

title: Explain closures in JavaScript with an example.
answer: A closure is a function that retains access to variables from its enclosing scope even after that outer function has finished executing, because JavaScript functions carry their lexical scope with them.

title: What is the 'this' keyword, and how does it differ in regular functions vs arrow functions?
answer: In a regular function, 'this' is determined by how the function is called (its call-site) — it can refer to the global object, undefined in strict mode, or the calling object. An arrow function doesn't have its own 'this'; it lexically inherits 'this' from its enclosing scope at definition time.

title: What is event bubbling and event capturing?
answer: Event bubbling means an event triggered on a nested element propagates upward through its ancestors. Event capturing is the opposite phase, where the event travels from the outermost ancestor down to the target element first. Most listeners default to the bubbling phase unless capturing is explicitly enabled.

title: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous code, and how do Promises help?
answer: Synchronous code runs line by line, blocking further execution until the current line finishes. Asynchronous code (like network requests) runs without blocking the main thread. A Promise represents a value that may not be available yet, providing .then()/.catch() handlers to manage success and failure cleanly instead of nested callbacks.

title: What is the difference between async/await and Promises?
answer: async/await is syntactic sugar built on top of Promises that lets asynchronous code read like synchronous code, improving readability, especially for sequential asynchronous steps, while still being backed by the same Promise mechanics underneath.

title: What is the difference between map(), filter(), and reduce()?
answer: map() transforms each element and returns a new array of the same length. filter() returns a new array containing only elements that pass a test. reduce() accumulates all elements into a single value based on a reducer function.
example:
const nums = [1, 2, 3, 4];
const doubled = nums.map(n => n * 2);      // [2,4,6,8]
const evens = nums.filter(n => n % 2 === 0); // [2,4]
const sum = nums.reduce((acc, n) => acc + n, 0); // 10

title: What is destructuring in JavaScript?
answer: Destructuring lets you unpack values from arrays or properties from objects into distinct variables in a single concise statement, reducing repetitive access code.
example:
const { name, age } = { name: 'Asha', age: 22 };
const [first, second] = [10, 20];

title: Explain the JavaScript event loop and how it handles asynchronous operations.
answer: JavaScript is single-threaded and uses an event loop to manage asynchronous work. The call stack executes synchronous code; async operations (timers, network calls) are handed off to Web APIs, and their callbacks are queued — microtasks (Promises) in the microtask queue, macrotasks (setTimeout) in the task queue. The event loop pushes queued callbacks onto the call stack only once it's empty, and microtasks are always drained before the next macrotask.

title: What is the prototype chain in JavaScript?
answer: Every JavaScript object has an internal link to a prototype object, forming a chain. When a property or method isn't found on an object, JavaScript looks up the chain through each prototype until it's found or the chain ends at null — this is how inheritance works in JavaScript without classical classes.

title: What is debouncing and throttling, and how do they differ?
answer: Debouncing delays executing a function until a specified time has passed since the last call, useful for search-input handlers to avoid firing on every keystroke. Throttling ensures a function executes at most once per specified time interval, regardless of how many times it's triggered, useful for scroll or resize event handlers.

title: What is the difference between call, apply, and bind?
answer: call() invokes a function immediately with a given 'this' and arguments passed individually. apply() does the same but takes arguments as an array. bind() doesn't invoke the function immediately — it returns a new function with 'this' permanently bound, callable later.

title: What are Web Workers and why would you use one?
answer: Web Workers run JavaScript in a background thread separate from the main UI thread, letting you perform CPU-intensive tasks (like heavy computation or data processing) without blocking the page from rendering or responding to user input, communicating with the main thread via message passing.

title: What is memoization, and how would you implement it?
answer: Memoization caches the results of expensive function calls based on their input, returning the cached result on repeated calls with the same input instead of recomputing, trading memory for speed.
